project_overview.project_development_objective: To increase equitable access to primary and secondary education, particularly for girls, in selected lagging provinces, and to improve learning conditions in Afghanistan
project_overview.total_financing: US$298 million (IDA US$100 million, ARTF US$100 million, GPE US$98 million)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Approximately 7 million children attending school regularly, with focus on out-of-school children in 17 lagging provinces, teachers, principals, and MoE staff

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: Yes - Afghanistan is classified as a fragile state with ongoing conflict. The document explicitly notes deteriorating security situation affecting education services, with some provinces having high security incidence and violence rates. However, the report indicates that within insecure provinces, schools do operate in some districts, and security impact is complex and varies by location

key_components.description: School infrastructure improvements (construction of 1,946 new school buildings and missing essential elements for 700 schools), school grants program for eligible schools, and community-based education expansion
Subject knowledge training for teachers, classroom observation and coaching pilots, curriculum reform, and textbook development and distribution

key_components.component_name: Strengthening Education Sector Planning Capacity and Transparency
key_components.description: EMIS improvements, teacher recruitment and management reforms, budget planning improvements, and results-based reporting
Capacity building for MoE, early childhood education pilot, and third-party verification of results

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Education (MoE) and Ministry of Rural Rehabilitation and Development (MRRD). MoE responsible for most components; MRRD responsible for school construction under Component 1
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: National Education Plan Steering Committee chaired by MoE; Task Force with representatives from MoE and MRRD; Provincial Coordination Committees; Joint working group with development partners
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations; Standard Procurement Documents; National Competitive Bidding for most contracts; community-driven procurement through CDCs for school construction; STEP system for tracking
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: EMIS strengthening with monthly phone surveys and physical verification; third-party verification agency for DLI achievement; internal audit by MoE and MRRD; external audit by Supreme Audit Office; Results Framework with PDO and intermediate indicators

identified_risks.risk_description: Recurrent security incidents causing disruption of government functioning and school operations; poor governance and accountability throughout administrative chain; widespread politicization and corruption in teacher recruitment; lack of clarity on responsibility and accountability
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Component 3 focuses on governance reforms; strengthening transparency through EMIS; implementing teacher policy with transparent recruitment process; establishing performance management system for PEDs and DEDs; community oversight through school shuras; third-party monitoring

identified_risks.risk_description: Low budget credibility with development budget execution at only 50% of budgeted amount; uncertainty about budget availability; declining aid and fiscal pressures; poverty increased from 38.3% to 54.5%
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: DLI-based disbursements incentivize government to allocate budget; one-time advance of US$18.5 million provided; norm-based budgeting to improve allocation; project design allows 75% of grant to follow traditional IPF modality

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Low confidence in program monitoring and reliability of administrative data; high number of schools to be constructed (2,646) in short timeframe; MRRD has not implemented such large construction project; only 1,200 schools have available land; CCAP operating in only 54 of 185 districts
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: EMIS strengthening with data verification procedures; third-party monitoring; MRRD to hire 151 additional engineers; use of CDC implementation model from CCAP; close monitoring at end of year 1 to adjust targets; ARTF third-party monitoring program

identified_risks.risk_description: Weaknesses in FM at MoE and MRRD; limited effectiveness of internal audit; non-implementation of audit recommendations; outstanding advances of AFN 85 million; no proper maintenance of fixed asset records; timeliness and quality of financial reporting concerns
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Detailed FM manual with internal control framework; strengthened internal audit capacity; semiannual internal audit reports; external audit by Supreme Audit Office; inventory and assets module development; integration of personnel database with payroll; third-party verification; community oversight

identified_risks.risk_category: Technical Design of Project or Program
identified_risks.risk_description: MoE lacks experience with RBF modality; concerns about predictability of resource availability; high reliance on donor dependence for development budget
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Process-based DLIs in initial phase; one-time advance to provide predictability; strong implementation support; technical assistance for capacity building

identified_risks.risk_description: Deteriorating security situation affecting school operations and government capacity to monitor; restricted mobility limiting World Bank supervision; lack of government presence in large parts of country
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Use of ARTF third-party monitoring program; flexible targeting of specific districts within provinces; community mobilization and awareness programs; alternative education through CBE in conflict-affected areas

identified_risks.risk_description: Widespread corruption in education sector including ghost teachers, students, and schools; corruption in teacher recruitment; lack of transparent resource allocation systems; Afghanistan ranked 177th of 180 in Corruption Perception Index
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Personnel database with biometric data to eliminate ghost workers; transparent teacher recruitment using Kankor methodology; wide publication of procurement information; grievance redress mechanism; community oversight through school shuras; third-party verification; internal audit strengthening

safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Project includes robust GRM with multiple uptake channels including hotline, awareness raising, registration and monitoring of grievances, and capacity building of agencies to address concerns. GRM functioning is monitored as a key indicator
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Project specifically targets girls' education with focus on 17 provinces with highest out-of-school girls; construction prioritizes girls' and mixed schools; gender-segregated latrines and boundary walls; school grants for female hygiene products and transport for female students and teachers; target of 40% female teacher recruitment; gender-disaggregated PDO indicators
vulnerable_groups: Persons with physical disabilities (schools designed to be accessible)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly addresses conflict context through: (1) CBE as alternative education in conflict-affected areas; (2) focus on provinces with security concerns; (3) flexible targeting allowing adjustment based on security situation; (4) curriculum reform emphasizing national identity and peace building; (5) community mobilization for social cohesion; (6) recognition that security impact is complex and varies by location
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Afghanistan faces multiple fragility factors: (1) deteriorating security with withdrawal of international forces; (2) weak institutions and governance; (3) high poverty (54.5%); (4) widespread corruption; (5) limited private sector development; (6) aid dependence; (7) political instability with election cycles; (8) low institutional capacity; (9) limited government presence in large parts of country. Project designed to strengthen state effectiveness and build social capital through education
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Project acknowledges that insecurity affects education services but impact is complex - some boys' schools operate even in volatile districts while in safer areas cultural/religious beliefs restrict girls' attendance. Project supports: (1) female-friendly infrastructure (boundary walls, gender-segregated latrines); (2) female teacher recruitment; (3) social mobilization campaigns; (4) CBE as alternative in insecure areas; (5) flexible geographic targeting; (6) third-party monitoring to overcome access constraints
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: No specific mention of refugee or IDP populations, though document notes that CBE is important means of education in conflict-affected areas and sparsely populated areas. Resettlement Policy Framework triggered for land acquisition for school construction, with procedures for managing land disputes and asset loss
